questions.question:
Assertion (A): The length of a tangent drawn from an external point to a circle is always equal to the radius of the circle.
Reason (R): The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

steps: Check Assertion: The length of a tangent from an external point is not necessarily equal to the radius; they are independent.
Check Reason: This is a standard theorem (Theorem 10.1 of NCERT).
Conclusion: Assertion is false, Reason is true.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): If the distance between the centres of two circles of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ is $d$, then they touch each other externally if $d = r_1 + r_2$.
Reason (R): The distance between the centres of two circles is the sum of their radii when they touch internally.

steps: Check Assertion: If two circles touch externally, the distance between their centres is indeed the sum of their radii. True.
Check Reason: If two circles touch internally, the distance between their centres is $|r_1 - r_2|$. False.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The number of tangents that can be drawn from a point inside a circle is zero.
Reason (R): A tangent to a circle intersects the circle at only one point.

steps: Check Assertion: Points inside the circle cannot have tangents drawn to the circle. True.
Check Reason: By definition, a tangent touches the circle at exactly one point. True.
Conclusion: Reason explains why no tangent can be drawn from an internal point.
solution.derivation: A: True, R: True, R explains A

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The length of the tangent from a point $P$ at a distance of $10 \text{ cm}$ from the centre of a circle of radius $6 \text{ cm}$ is $8 \text{ cm}$.
Reason (R): In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.

steps: Check Reason: Pythagoras theorem is used to calculate the tangent length. True.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Two parallel tangents can be drawn to a circle at the ends of a diameter.
Reason (R): Tangents at the ends of a diameter are perpendicular to the diameter.

steps: Check Assertion: Tangents at ends of diameter are parallel. True.

Check Reason: Since the radius is perpendicular to the tangent at the point of contact, and the diameter is a straight line, the tangents are perpendicular to the diameter and thus parallel to each other. True.
